label: Playground Mono
description: Dark developer-flavored Next.js starter with a monospace heading face and a calm prototype-shell vibe. Best when the prompt sounds like a sandbox, internal tool, or open-ended dev playground rather than a finished product.
signatureMotif: calm dark playground shell, mono headings, and prototype-friendly chrome
promptHints: Treat the page as a working developer playground or sandbox shell, not a marketed product surface.
Use mono headings and small monospaced labels to signal that this is scaffolding ready for code, not a finished brand.
Keep one calm primary accent (cool blue) and let dark surfaces, hairline borders, and tabs do the visual work.

layouts: Use a centered single-column shell with a thin top toolbar that reads like a tab strip, a compact title block, and content panels separated by subtle dividers.
Build the hero as a left-aligned mono headline, a short body line, and a small command-style preview block beside or below the primary action.
Compose feature or section areas as a two-column grid of dark cards with small mono labels in the corner and short descriptive copy underneath.
Lay out docs or settings pages with a narrow left rail, sticky section anchors, and a calm reading column that mimics a working IDE side panel.
Use forms and config screens as stacked dark cards with key-value rows, inline helper text, and a single bright primary button anchoring the bottom.
motifs: Pair near-black graphite surfaces with hairline cool borders, a single cool blue primary, and small mono labels that feel like devtool chrome.
Use mono for headings, tab labels, and metric callouts; switch to a clean sans body so paragraphs still read like a real product page.
Cards should feel like inspectable panels: subtle inner highlights, 0.6–0.75rem corners, and content density slightly higher than a marketing page.
Let interactivity read as utilitarian — calm focus rings, restrained hovers, and small chips for status rather than glossy buttons.
antiPatterns: Avoid full-bleed marketing heroes, gradient lifestyle backgrounds, or oversized brand statements that turn the playground into a launch page.
Never lean on neon glows, dramatic accent washes, or terminal-cosplay green-on-black; this variant stays calm and prototype-grade.
Avoid playful illustrations, emoji-heavy chrome, or pastel cards that erase the developer-tool reading and reduce extension flexibility.
Do not stack multiple accent colors or fill the page with dense dashboard widgets before the prompt clearly asks for an operational dashboard.
